title: What the Interpreter Carries
summary: A veteran courtroom interpreter and a younger linguist disagree about where her job really ends — and whether faithfulness to the words can betray the person speaking them.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: You did beautifully today, Marko. Every term rendered exactly. The judge could have read a transcript and missed nothing.

body.speaker: Marko
body.text: Thank you. Though I confess I left feeling I had failed the man. He was terrified, and I gave the court a calm, precise account of his terror. Something didn't survive the crossing.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: But that is precisely the discipline, isn't it? We are not advocates. The moment we soften a witness, or add a tremble he didn't put there, we have stopped translating and started inventing. The words are not ours to improve, however much we wish they were kinder.

body.speaker: Marko
body.text: I don't dispute the principle. What unsettles me is how much rides on things the words never hold. When he said the village was 'quiet', he meant the silence after the soldiers left — not peace, but the absence of anyone left to speak. I rendered 'quiet'. The court heard calm.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: So you would have me editorialise — tell the bench what he 'really' meant. But who appointed us to know that? Thirty years in this booth have taught me that the instant I trust my reading of a heart over the words on the page, I become a translator who thinks she is a mind-reader.

body.speaker: Marko
body.text: You're right that certainty is the trap. Yet there is a narrower thing I mean. A pause, a stumble. A man chooses a humble word where a proud one was available, and that choice is information. To flatten every register into neutral legal English is itself a distortion — just an invisible one we've agreed to ignore.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Now that I'll grant you. Register is part of the message, not decoration on it. We were taught to be panes of glass — transparent, absent. But glass has thickness; it bends the light a little whether we admit it or not. The honest interpreter names her own refraction and keeps it small.

body.speaker: Marko
body.text: That's it, exactly. Not invisibility, but honesty about the bending. So when I match his hesitation with a hesitation of my own, I'm not inventing — I'm refusing to erase what was already there.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Provided you can defend it. That's the test I give the young ones: could you justify this choice to the speaker himself, in his own language, and have him nod? If you're guessing at a meaning to make him sympathetic, stop. Sympathy is the court's job, not ours.

body.speaker: Marko
body.text: Then we agree more than I feared. You guard against the interpreter who rewrites; I grieve for the one who amputates. Both of us are trying to deliver the whole man — you by trusting his words, me by trusting his silences.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: And both of us will get it wrong on a bad day. The work isn't to be perfect; it's to know which way we each tend to fail, and lean against it. You'll add what isn't there; I'll strip what is. A good booth needs one of each, watching the other.

annotations.meaningVi: dịch lại một cách chính xác
nỗi kinh hoàng, sự sợ hãi tột độ
không còn nguyên vẹn khi chuyển qua
người bênh vực, luật sư biện hộ
làm dịu đi, làm nhẹ bớt
không phải việc của ta để chỉnh sửa cho hay hơn
tôi không phản đối nguyên tắc đó
bao nhiêu thứ phụ thuộc vào

annotations.phrase: the absence of anyone left to speak
annotations.meaningVi: sự vắng mặt của bất kỳ ai còn sống để lên tiếng
annotations.pronunciation: /ði ˈæbsəns əv ˈɛniwʌn lɛft tə spiːk/

annotations.meaningVi: thêm thắt ý kiến chủ quan
hội đồng xét xử, thẩm phán
sự chắc chắn chính là cái bẫy
phong cách ngôn ngữ, sắc thái lời nói
tái hiện sự do dự của ông ấy
miễn là anh có thể bảo vệ lựa chọn đó

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence for emphasis ('What... is')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ 'What unsettles me is how much rides on things the words never hold' đẩy thông tin chính ra trước để nhấn mạnh điều khiến nhân vật bất an.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The cleft 'What unsettles me is...' front-loads and emphasises the speaker's central concern.

grammarNotes.title: Concession then counter ('I don't dispute... What unsettles me...')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Marko thừa nhận một phần ('I don't dispute the principle') rồi mới đưa ra điểm phản biện hẹp hơn — kiểu nhượng bộ rồi vặn lại điển hình ở trình độ B2.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Each speaker concedes a true point before narrowing to a counter-claim, a hallmark of nuanced B2 argument.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional with inverted-style 'Provided (that)'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Provided you can defend it' = 'as long as / on condition that you can defend it' — một liên từ điều kiện trang trọng thay cho 'if'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Provided (that)' introduces a condition more formally than 'if'.

translationVi:
Priya: Hôm nay anh làm rất xuất sắc trong phòng xử, Marko. Từng thuật ngữ đều được dịch chính xác. Thẩm phán có đọc lại biên bản cũng chẳng bỏ sót điều gì.
Marko: Cảm ơn chị. Dù tôi phải thú nhận rằng khi rời phòng tôi thấy mình đã phụ người đàn ông ấy. Ông ấy hoảng sợ, vậy mà tôi lại trao cho tòa một bản tường thuật điềm tĩnh, chính xác về nỗi sợ của ông. Có gì đó đã không còn nguyên vẹn khi chuyển qua.
Priya: Nhưng đó chính là nguyên tắc của nghề, phải không? Chúng ta không phải người biện hộ. Khoảnh khắc ta làm dịu lời một nhân chứng, hay thêm vào một sự run rẩy mà ông ấy không hề có, là ta đã thôi dịch và bắt đầu bịa đặt. Những lời ấy không phải của ta để chỉnh cho hay hơn.
Marko: Tôi không phản đối nguyên tắc đó. Điều khiến tôi bất an là bao nhiêu thứ phụ thuộc vào những điều mà ngôn từ chẳng bao giờ chứa nổi. Khi ông ấy nói ngôi làng 'yên tĩnh', ông muốn nói sự im lặng sau khi lính rút đi — không phải bình yên, mà là sự vắng mặt của bất kỳ ai còn sống để lên tiếng. Tôi dịch là 'yên tĩnh'. Tòa nghe ra sự điềm tĩnh.
Priya: Vậy là anh muốn tôi thêm thắt ý chủ quan. Nói cho hội đồng biết ông ấy 'thật sự' muốn nói gì. Nhưng ai cho phép ta biết điều đó? Ba mươi năm trong cabin này đã dạy tôi rằng khoảnh khắc tôi tin vào cách mình đọc một trái tim hơn là những lời trên trang giấy, tôi trở thành người nguy hiểm nhất trong phòng — một thông dịch viên tưởng mình đọc được suy nghĩ.
Marko: Chị đúng khi nói sự chắc chắn chính là cái bẫy. Nhưng có một điều hẹp hơn mà tôi muốn nói. Một quãng ngừng. Một người chọn từ khiêm nhường trong khi có sẵn từ kiêu hãnh, và lựa chọn đó là thông tin. Làm phẳng mọi sắc thái thành 'tiếng Anh pháp lý trung tính' tự nó là một sự bóp méo — chỉ là sự bóp méo vô hình mà ta đã đồng thuận làm ngơ.
Priya: Điều đó thì tôi công nhận. Sắc thái lời nói là một phần của thông điệp, không phải đồ trang trí trên nó. Khi tôi mới vào nghề, người ta dạy chúng tôi phải là những tấm kính — trong suốt, vô hình. Nhưng kính có độ dày; nó làm khúc xạ ánh sáng đôi chút dù ta có thừa nhận hay không. Người thông dịch trung thực không giả vờ mình vô hình. Cô ấy gọi tên sự khúc xạ của chính mình và giữ nó thật nhỏ.
Marko: Đúng vậy, chính xác. Không phải vô hình, mà là trung thực về sự khúc xạ. Nên khi tôi tái hiện sự do dự của ông ấy bằng một sự do dự của chính mình, tôi không bịa đặt — tôi từ chối xóa bỏ điều vốn đã ở đó.
Priya: Miễn là anh bảo vệ được nó. Đó là phép thử tôi trao cho lớp trẻ: anh có thể biện minh cho lựa chọn này với chính người nói, bằng ngôn ngữ của ông ấy, và khiến ông gật đầu không? Nếu có, hãy dịch như vậy. Nếu anh đang đoán một ý nghĩa để khiến ông ấy đáng thương hơn, hãy dừng lại. Lòng trắc ẩn là việc của tòa, không phải của ta.
Marko: Vậy thì chúng ta đồng ý với nhau nhiều hơn tôi tưởng. Chị đề phòng kẻ thông dịch viết lại; tôi xót xa cho kẻ cắt bỏ. Cả hai chúng ta đều cố trao đi trọn vẹn con người ấy — chị bằng cách tin vào lời ông ấy, tôi bằng cách tin vào những khoảng lặng của ông.
Priya: Và cả hai ta đều sẽ sai vào một ngày tồi tệ. Công việc không phải là hoàn hảo; mà là biết mình thường sai theo hướng nào, và tự đề kháng lại nó. Anh sẽ thêm vào điều không có ở đó; tôi sẽ tước đi điều vốn có. Một cabin tốt cần mỗi loại một người, canh chừng lẫn nhau.

questions.prompt: Why does Marko feel he 'failed the man' despite Priya's praise?
options: He mistranslated several legal terms during the hearing.
He arrived late and missed part of the testimony.
He delivered an accurate but emotionally flattened account of the witness's fear.
He openly argued with the judge in the courtroom.
questions.explanation: Marko says he 'gave the court a calm, precise account of his terror' and that 'something didn't survive the crossing'.
questions.hint: Look at Marko's first reply, after Priya praises him.

questions.prompt: What does Priya mean by saying interpreters are 'not advocates'?
options: Interpreters should never speak in court at all.
Interpreters must not argue for or soften a witness's case.
questions.explanation: She warns that softening a witness or adding a tremble means 'we have stopped translating and started inventing'.
questions.hint: See the reply where Priya first defends 'the discipline'.

questions.prompt: What did the witness's word 'quiet' actually convey, according to Marko?
options: The silence left because no one remained alive to speak.
Genuine peace returning to the village.
That the soldiers had been polite and orderly.
That the village had always been calm.
questions.explanation: Marko explains he meant 'not peace, but the absence of anyone left to speak'.
questions.hint: Marko unpacks a single word he rendered as 'quiet'.

questions.prompt: Why does Priya call herself 'the most dangerous person in the room' in one scenario?
options: When she falls asleep during long hearings.
When she trusts her own reading of a speaker's heart over the actual words.
When she refuses to translate offensive language.
When she corrects the judge's grammar.
questions.explanation: She says the danger is 'the instant I trust my reading of a heart over the words on the page'.
questions.hint: Priya describes a 'translator who thinks she is a mind-reader'.

questions.prompt: What narrower point does Marko make about a speaker's word choice?
options: That word choice is random and carries no meaning.
That all witnesses should be coached to speak formally.
That choosing a humble word over a proud one is itself information worth preserving.
That legal English is always the most accurate register.
questions.explanation: Marko says 'a man chooses a humble word where a proud one was available, and that choice is information'.
questions.hint: Look where Marko mentions a pause and a humble word.

questions.prompt: What does Priya concede with the metaphor of glass having 'thickness'?
options: That interpreters should be replaced by recordings.
That glass booths should be made thinner.
That no interpreter is truly invisible; she inevitably bends the message a little.
questions.explanation: She admits 'glass has thickness; it bends the light a little whether we admit it or not'.
questions.hint: Priya revises the old training image of being a 'pane of glass'.

questions.prompt: Which idea do BOTH speakers ultimately agree on?
options: That register is part of the message, and honesty about one's own influence matters more than pretending to be invisible.
That interpreters should freely add emotion to make witnesses sympathetic.
That accuracy of legal terms is the only thing that counts.
That interpreters should give legal advice to witnesses.
questions.explanation: Priya grants 'register is part of the message, not decoration', and both move toward 'honesty about the bending'.
questions.hint: Notice where the disagreement narrows into shared ground.

questions.prompt: What is Priya's practical test for whether a rendering is legitimate?
options: Whether the judge approves of it afterwards.
Whether the interpreter could justify it to the speaker in his own language and have him nod.
Whether it makes the witness sound more sympathetic.
Whether it uses neutral legal English.
questions.explanation: She tells the young ones: 'could you justify this choice to the speaker himself, in his own language, and have him nod?'
questions.hint: Priya gives 'the young ones' a specific test.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the writer NOT claiming through this dialogue?
options: That faithful translation can still lose part of a speaker's meaning.
That register and silence can carry genuine information.
That interpreters should freely guess a speaker's hidden meaning to make him sympathetic.
That interpreters tend to fail in opposite, predictable ways.
questions.explanation: Priya explicitly warns against guessing 'a meaning to make him sympathetic' — 'Sympathy is the court's job, not ours.'
questions.hint: One option contradicts Priya's final warning; the others are supported.

questions.prompt: How does Marko distinguish 'matching hesitation' from inventing?
options: He claims any addition is acceptable if the judge agrees.
He admits it is simply a form of acting.
He believes hesitation should always be removed for clarity.
He says reproducing a real hesitation refuses to erase what was already there, rather than adding something new.
questions.explanation: Marko says 'I'm not inventing — I'm refusing to erase what was already there.'
questions.hint: See Marko's reply just after Priya's 'refraction' point.

questions.prompt: How do the two speakers describe their characteristic ways of failing?
options: Marko adds what isn't there; Priya strips away what is.
Both tend to add emotion that isn't present.
Both tend to omit legal terms.
Priya adds detail; Marko removes it.
questions.explanation: Priya says: 'You'll add what isn't there; I'll strip what is.'
questions.hint: Look at Priya's very last line about how each tends to fail.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase 'didn't survive the crossing' metaphorically refer to?
options: A dangerous river journey the witness described.
The emotional reality lost when fear is rendered into precise words.
The transcript being damaged in transit.
questions.explanation: Marko uses it to describe how the man's terror was not preserved when translated into 'a calm, precise account'.
questions.hint: It appears right after Marko mentions the man's terror.

questions.prompt: Why is the dialogue NOT a simple 'one says yes, one says no' debate?
options: Because neither speaker says anything substantive.
Because they are discussing two unrelated jobs.
Because each acknowledges a true point the other makes and they converge on shared principles.
Because the judge settles the argument for them.
questions.explanation: Priya concedes register matters; Marko concedes certainty is a trap, and Marko says 'we agree more than I feared.'
questions.hint: Track the concessions each speaker grants the other.

questions.prompt: What does Priya mean by 'Sympathy is the court's job, not ours'?
options: Interpreters should feel nothing for witnesses.
Courts are always sympathetic to witnesses.
Deciding how to feel about a witness belongs to the court, not the interpreter's renderings.
questions.explanation: She draws a line: shaping sympathy is not the interpreter's role; rendering faithfully is.
questions.hint: Read the end of Priya's 'test for the young ones' reply.

questions.prompt: What conclusion does Priya reach about working in a booth?
options: A good booth pairs interpreters with opposite tendencies so each watches the other.
Interpreters should always work alone for accuracy.
Interpreters should aim for perfection and never make mistakes.
Booths should be eliminated in favour of written transcripts.
questions.explanation: She concludes 'A good booth needs one of each, watching the other.'
questions.hint: Her final sentence states what a good booth needs.

questions.prompt: What does the word 'amputates' suggest about Marko's fear?
options: That interpreters may injure witnesses physically.
That an interpreter can cut away living parts of a speaker's meaning, like removing a limb.
That the court removes interpreters from cases.
questions.explanation: Marko grieves for 'the one who amputates', contrasting it with Priya's fear of the one who 'rewrites'.
questions.hint: He uses it to name his own characteristic failure.

cloze.template:
Priya: You did beautifully in there today, Marko. Every term {{b1}} exactly. The judge could have read a transcript and missed nothing.
Marko: Thank you. Though I {{b2}} I left the room feeling I had failed the man. He was terrified, and I gave the court a calm, precise account of his {{b3}}. Something didn't survive the crossing.
Priya: But that is precisely the {{b4}}, isn't it? We are not {{b5}}. The moment we {{b6}} a witness, or add a tremble he didn't put there, we have stopped translating and started inventing.
Marko: I don't {{b7}} the principle. What unsettles me is how much {{b8}} on things the words never hold. When he said the village was 'quiet', he meant not peace, but the absence of anyone left to speak.
Priya: So you would have me {{b9}}. Tell the bench what he 'really' meant. But thirty years have taught me that the instant I trust my reading of a heart over the words, I become a translator who thinks she is a {{b10}}.
Marko: You're right that certainty is the {{b11}}. A man chooses a humble word where a proud one was available, and that choice is information. To flatten every {{b12}} into neutral legal English is itself a distortion.
Priya: Now that I'll grant you. When I started, we were taught to be panes of glass — {{b13}}, absent. But glass has thickness; it {{b14}} the light a little. The honest interpreter names her own {{b15}} and keeps it small.
Marko: So when I match his hesitation with a hesitation of my own, I'm not inventing — I'm refusing to {{b16}} what was already there.
Priya: Provided you can defend it. Could you justify this choice to the speaker himself, and have him nod? If you're guessing at a meaning to make him sympathetic, stop. {{b17}} is the court's job, not ours.
Marko: You guard against the interpreter who rewrites; I grieve for the one who {{b18}}.
Priya: The work isn't to be perfect; it's to know which way we each tend to fail, and {{b19}} against it. You'll add what isn't there; I'll {{b20}} what is.

blanks.explanation: 'rendered exactly' = translated precisely.
'I confess' admits a private feeling.
The man was terrified, so 'terror' fits.
'the discipline' names the professional rule.
Interpreters are 'not advocates' (not lawyers).
'soften a witness' = make his words gentler.
'I don't dispute the principle' = I don't argue against it.
'how much rides on' = how much depends on.
To 'editorialise' is to insert one's own interpretation.
A 'mind-reader' presumes to know hidden thoughts.
'certainty is the trap' warns against false confidence.
'register' = the style or tone of speech.
Panes of glass are meant to be 'transparent'.
Glass 'bends the light' — the refraction metaphor.
She names her own 'refraction' (bending of meaning).
He refuses to 'erase' what was already there.
'Sympathy is the court's job, not ours.'
Marko fears the interpreter who 'amputates' meaning.
'lean against it' = resist one's own tendency.
Priya tends to 'strip' away what is present.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marko trusts a speaker's silences; Priya trusts only his words. In a high-stakes setting — a courtroom, a hospital, an asylum interview — whose error would you rather risk: adding meaning that wasn't said, or removing meaning that was? Defend your choice with an example.
